questions.q: "Smiling Buddha" was the code name for what event?
questions.explanation: Smiling Buddha was the code name of India's first nuclear weapon test, conducted in 1974.

questions.q: In which year was the Smiling Buddha test conducted?
questions.explanation: India detonated its first nuclear device on 18 May 1974.

questions.q: At which test site was Smiling Buddha conducted?
questions.explanation: The test took place at the Pokhran Test Range in the Thar Desert of Rajasthan.

questions.q: In which Indian state is the Pokhran test site located?
questions.explanation: Pokhran is in the Thar Desert region of Rajasthan.

questions.q: Smiling Buddha made India the member of which exclusive group of nations?
options: The first to land on the Moon
Nations that had detonated a nuclear device
questions.explanation: With Smiling Buddha, India became the sixth nation to successfully test a nuclear device.

questions.q: Which type of device was tested in Smiling Buddha?
questions.explanation: Smiling Buddha was a plutonium-based fission (implosion) device.

questions.q: What was the date of the Smiling Buddha test?
questions.explanation: The test was carried out on 18 May 1974, the day of Buddha Purnima.

questions.q: Who was the Prime Minister of India when Smiling Buddha was conducted?
questions.explanation: Indira Gandhi was Prime Minister and authorized the 1974 test.

questions.q: India officially described the Smiling Buddha test as what kind of explosion?
questions.explanation: India termed it a "peaceful nuclear explosion" (PNE).

questions.q: Smiling Buddha was India's nuclear test of what ordinal rank for the country?
questions.explanation: It was India's very first nuclear test, preceding the 1998 Pokhran-II series.

questions.q: The date 18 May 1974 coincided with which Buddhist festival, inspiring the code name?
options: Vesak eve only in Sri Lanka
questions.explanation: The test fell on Buddha Purnima (Buddha Jayanti), which inspired the name Smiling Buddha.

questions.q: Which fissile material was used in the Smiling Buddha device?
questions.explanation: The device used plutonium produced in the CIRUS research reactor.

questions.q: The plutonium for the test was largely produced in which research reactor?
questions.explanation: The CIRUS reactor at Trombay supplied the plutonium for the device.

questions.q: Which Indian organization led the design and execution of the Smiling Buddha device?
questions.explanation: The Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC) developed and assembled the device.

questions.q: Who was the director of BARC and a key scientific leader of the 1974 test?
questions.explanation: Raja Ramanna led the team that designed and tested the device.

questions.q: Approximately how was the Smiling Buddha device deployed for the test?
options: Detonated on a tower in open air
questions.explanation: The device was placed in a shaft about 100 metres deep and detonated underground.

questions.q: Which famous Indian nuclear physicist had founded the program but died in 1966, before the 1974 test?
questions.explanation: Homi J. Bhabha, founder of India's nuclear program, died in a 1966 plane crash.

questions.q: What was the reported coded message sent to Indira Gandhi to confirm the successful test?
questions.explanation: The success was reportedly confirmed with the phrase "The Buddha is smiling."

questions.q: A major international consequence of the 1974 test was the formation of which body?
options: The Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty Organization
questions.explanation: The test prompted the creation of the Nuclear Suppliers Group to control nuclear exports.

questions.q: The 1974 test placed strain on India's relations and led to sanctions especially affecting cooperation with which two countries?
questions.explanation: Canada (which supplied CIRUS) and the United States curtailed nuclear cooperation after the test.

questions.q: Which engineer-administrator headed the Atomic Energy Commission of India at the time of the 1974 test?
questions.explanation: Homi Sethna chaired the Atomic Energy Commission and oversaw the 1974 program.

questions.q: Pokhran, the site of Smiling Buddha, lies within which desert?
questions.explanation: Pokhran is located in the Thar Desert of western Rajasthan.

questions.q: What was the approximate official yield reported by India for the Smiling Buddha device?
questions.explanation: India officially reported a yield of about 12 kilotons, though independent estimates and later admissions put it lower (around 4-10 kilotons).

questions.q: The CIRUS reactor that produced the plutonium was built with assistance primarily from which country?
questions.explanation: CIRUS (Canada-India Reactor) was built with Canadian assistance, with the US supplying heavy water.

questions.q: Which physicist, later associated with later tests, was a young member of the 1974 device team and led work on its design aspects?
questions.explanation: P. K. Iyengar was a key member of the 1974 design team and later became AEC chairman.

questions.q: Roughly how deep was the shaft in which the Smiling Buddha device was emplaced?
questions.explanation: The device was placed at the bottom of a shaft approximately 100 metres deep.

questions.q: The 1974 test is sometimes also referred to internally by which other designation?
questions.explanation: It is retrospectively called Pokhran-I, distinguishing it from the 1998 Pokhran-II tests.

questions.q: Which nuclear physicist worked on the 1974 device and later led India's 1998 nuclear tests?
questions.explanation: Nuclear physicist Rajagopala Chidambaram worked on the 1974 device's design and later led the 1998 Pokhran-II tests.

questions.q: Smiling Buddha made India the first country outside which group to detonate a nuclear device?
options: The five permanent members of the UN Security Council (P5)
questions.explanation: India became the first nation outside the five recognized nuclear-weapon states (the UN P5) to test a device.

questions.q: Following the 1974 test, India did NOT conduct another nuclear test until which year?
questions.explanation: India's next nuclear tests were the Pokhran-II (Operation Shakti) series in 1998.
